coredata.dc:title: Lentils/urea as precursors for profound S and N doping of carbon dots for selective sequential determination of Hg(II) and S(II) in food and environmental samples
coredata.dc:creator: Abd Elhaleem, Shymaa M.
Belal, Fathalla
El-Shabrawy, Yasser
El-Maghrabey, Mahmoud
coredata.dc:description: In this article, we described a new platform for probing toxic pollutants, namely Hg2+ and S2-, in aquatic environment by tracing the “on–off-on” fluorescence signals of quantum dots. To achieve this, red lentils and urea, as natural precursors, were utilized as sources of S and N doping, respectively, to fabricate blue fluorescent carbon quantum dots, namely, lentils/urea quantum dots (LUQDs) with good quantum yield and plentiful S-doping. In the present work, the quality-by-design (QbD) strategy was applied to control and select the optimum conditions for the microwave-aided synthesis of the carbon dots. LUQDs exhibit excitation-dependent emissions, where the maximum fluorescence emission was at 360 nm after excitation at 300 nm. During the detection step, the fluorescence of LUQDs is quenched by the incorporation of Hg2+ over the range of 0.50–70.0 μM. Next, S2- reacts with Hg2+, resulting in turning on the fluorescence emission of LUQDs in the range of 10.0–150.0 µM.The method was applied for the detection of both Hg2+ and S2- in tap, sewage, and river water samples with a recovery rate ranging between 98.96 % and 101.26 %; in addition, Hg2+ was detected in canned Tuna samples with a recovery of 98.70 %. Furthermore, the greenness of the developed method was confirmed through various greenness metrics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Red lentils
Microwave synthesis
Mercury
Sulfide
Tuna

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence measurement of Hg2+ and S2-
Analysis of Hg2+ and S2- in water Samples
Analysis of Hg2+ in canned Tuna Fish
Experimental design in the fabrication of LUQDs
Analysis of Hg2+ and S2- in water samples
Analysis of Hg2+ in canned Tuna Fish

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is funded by Mansoura University (project code number: Mu-phar-23\u201332).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The 23 full factorial design main effect plots (A), full interaction plots (B), optimization plots for the synthesis of LUQDs (C), and Pareto chart of the effects on QY response at α = 0.05 (D).

(A) The typical TEM image of LUQDs and their size distribution (from 3 to 8 nm),(B) FT-IR spectrum of LUQDs, (C) EDX spectrum of as-synthesized LUQDs, (D) Elemental mapping of LUQDs, (E) XRD pattern of LUQDs, (F) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of LUQDs (a, a‘) (inset: LUQDs at normal light(left) and LUQDs at UV light(right)), and (G) Fluorescence emission spectra of LUQDs with different excitation wavelengths.

(A) Selectivity and interference of LUQDs towards Hg2+ in the presence of other common metals at pH 3, 5,7, and 8, (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of LUQDs in aqueous solution upon addition of various concentrations of Hg2+ (from top to bottom: 0 μM, 0.5 μM, 10.0 μM, 20.0 μM, 30.0 μM, 40.0 μM, 50.0 μM, 60.0 μM, and 70.0 μM), and (C) Linear regression plot of relative fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration in concentration range of 0.5–70.0 μM.

(A) Effect of Hg2+ concentration on the recovery of the fluorescence intensity of LUQDs upon addition of 30.0 μM S2-, (B) Selectivity and interference of LUQDs towards S2- in the presence of other common species. The fluorescence intensities of LUQDs without and with S2- at 360 nm, respectively, (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of LUQDs-Hg2+ solution upon addition of various concentrations of S2- (from bottom to top: 0 μM, 10.0 μM, 30.0 μM, 50.0 μM, 70.0 μM, 90.0 μM, 130.0 μM, and 150.0 μM), and (D) Linear regression plot of relative fluorescence intensity and S2- concentration in concentration range of 10.0–150.0 μM.
Schematic representation of the sensing mechanism of the LUQDs as an “on–off-on” fluorescent sensor for the detection of Hg (II) and sulfide.
General procedure for synthesis and application of LUQDs for Hg2+ and S2- sensing.
Figures of merit for some of the formerly reported CQD-based Hg(II) fluorescence sensors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the proposed method with reported CQD-based S (II) fluorescence sensors.

row.entry: Flower petals from Rosa indica + ethylenediamine + l-cysteine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of the proposed method for the determination of Hg2+ and S2- in environmental water samples and determination of Hg2+ in canned Tuna fish.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each result is the average of three separate determinations.

head.ce:title: Lentils/urea as precursors for profound S and N doping of carbon dots for selective sequential determination of Hg(II) and S(II) in food and environmental samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, 35516, Mansoura, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, 35516, Mansoura, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry for Pharmaceuticals, Course of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences, Nagasaki University, 1-14 Bunkyo-machi, Nagasaki 852-8521, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry for Pharmaceuticals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry for Pharmaceuticals, Course of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences, Nagasaki University, 1-14 Bunkyo-machi, Nagasaki 852-8521, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, we described a new platform for probing toxic pollutants, namely Hg2+ and S2-, in aquatic environment by tracing the “on–off-on” fluorescence signals of quantum dots. To achieve this, red lentils and urea, as natural precursors, were utilized as sources of S and N doping, respectively, to fabricate blue fluorescent carbon quantum dots, namely, lentils/urea quantum dots (LUQDs) with good quantum yield and plentiful S-doping. In the present work, the quality-by-design (QbD) strategy was applied to control and select the optimum conditions for the microwave-aided synthesis of the carbon dots. LUQDs exhibit excitation-dependent emissions, where the maximum fluorescence emission was at 360 nm after excitation at 300 nm. During the detection step, the fluorescence of LUQDs is quenched by the incorporation of Hg2+ over the range of 0.50–70.0 μM. Next, S2- reacts with Hg2+, resulting in turning on the fluorescence emission of LUQDs in the range of 10.0–150.0 µM.The method was applied for the detection of both Hg2+ and S2- in tap, sewage, and river water samples with a recovery rate ranging between 98.96 % and 101.26 %; in addition, Hg2+ was detected in canned Tuna samples with a recovery of 98.70 %. Furthermore, the greenness of the developed method was confirmed through various greenness metrics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have been considered to be one of the most important fluorescent carbon nanoparticles that play a vital role in human life because of their practical and essential applications. CDs have attracted attention in nano-chemistry due to their unique and unusual physicochemical properties, including low cost, high biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, low cytotoxicity, abundant functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, amino, carboxyl, and carbonyl), ease of production, water solubility, exceptional stability, and electron mobility [1–5]. Recently, more concern has been given to the green synthesis of CDs from renewable natural sources (fruits, seeds, animals, and waste). Green precursors are favorable owing to their high carbon content, lack of toxicity, availability, extreme stability, eco-friendliness, and affordability [6]. CDs have broad applications in several areas, including the industry of solar cells,energy conversion devices, nanomedicine, drug delivery, disease detection, bioimaging environmental monitoring, biosensing, and photocatalysis [7].Numerous overviews of the usage of CDs in the assay and removal of pollutants applications have been reported. On the experimental scale, CDs offer high ability in theestimationoftoxic pollutantsin the aquatic environment with various types, including bacteria (E. coli,Salmonella), anions (NO2−, S2−, ….….etc.), several metal ions (Fe3+, Cr6+, Cu2+, …) and other compounds (antibiotics, pesticides, …), achieving diverse detection limits [6]. CDs are effective adsorbents due to the presence of a lot of negatively charged surface groups, involving hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which generate operative electrostatic interaction with cationic pollutants along with offering active sites for reversible adsorption and desorption processes [8]. Our research team shows a keen interest in manufacturing CDs regarding their incredible and amazing merits and has made good use of their utilities in analyzing various analytes. These analytes may be drugs, pesticides, toxic metals, and natural ingredients [9,10].

Presently, heavy metals are considered highly toxic chemical components. Heavy metal ions originate from fertilizers, by-products of cosmetics, and industrial effluents. They may cause harmful effects on aquatic organisms and human health, resulting from their high accumulation tendency and persistence. Their toxicity is related to bond formation between metals and functional groups of proteins, such as the thiol group, resulting in alteration of the biochemical life cycle in the cell. Among metals that cause toxicity even at low concentrations, Hg2+ is extensively distributed in numerous environmental media (air, water, and soil) and foodstuffs (e.g., Tuna fish) [11]. Historically, Hg2+ was utilized in the manufacture of dyes and is still broadly used in electronic devices, glass mercury thermometers, and industrial catalysis [12]. Numerous analytical approaches have been used for quantitative detection of Hg (II) in water and fish samples, viz: electrochemical techniques [13,14], cold vapor atomic absorption spectroscopy[15], colorimetry [16], mass-spectrometry[17], inductively-coupled plasma spectroscopy[18] and several spectrofluorimetric techniques[19–21].

On the other hand, sulfide (S2-) is an environmental toxicant produced from natural and industrial sources[22]. At low micromolar concentrations, S2- causes acute toxicity in most susceptible organisms owing to its ability to diffuse across biological membranes. S2- reversibly binds to Cytochrome C oxidase (COX), the terminal enzyme in the electron transport chain, leading to inhibition of oxidative phosphorylation. Additionally, S2- may also cause impaired blood oxygen transport through the partial or complete exchange of oxyhemoglobin (oxyHb) to sulfhemoglobin (SHb) [23]. The elevated amount of S2- in water has been a significant environmental concern. Quantitative detection of S2- toxicity in water has become a topic of interest to the community. Various conventional analytical techniques have been performed for the estimation of S2- in environmental water samples, including electrochemical approaches [24–26], inductively-coupled plasma spectrometry [27], electrogenerated chemiluminescence[28], colorimetry [29] and gas chromatography [30].

As Hg2+ is well known to react strongly with sulfides (Solubility Product, Ksp = 1 x 10 -35), we aim to develop sulfide-rich CDs that could interact with Hg2+ reversibly to be used as a platform for Hg2+ and S2- monitoring. Lentil is a kind of edible pulse that ranks fifth in total global production. Among the five main types of lentils, red lentils are the main varieties produced worldwide.Lentils are very rich in sulfide and have high amounts of protein, sulfur amino acids, minerals, and complex hydrocarbons, making them excellent precursors for S-Doped CQDs; especially, they are safe and even have a great influence in reducing the risk of coronary heart diseases, obesity, and type 2 diabetes [31]. Additionally, chemical fertilizers have played a crucial role in increasing food production around the world. Urea manufacturing is the backbone of fertilizer industries. It is also employed as an initial molecule to produce plastics and drugs[32].

Here, we aimed to produce sulfur-rich nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from lentils and urea, namely lentils/urea quantum dots (LUQDs), adopting a quality-by-design (QbD) approach to implement the design of experiments (DoE). Owing to their rich content in sulfur amino acids, red lentils [31] were used for profound S-doping of quantum dots using a microwave synthesis approach; however, the obtained QDs have poor quantum yield. Next, we used a fertilizer, urea, for N-doping of red lentils with heavily S-doped quantum dots to achieve a high quantum yield, which was proved to be successful. Adapting the QbD approach offers tremendous advantages in achieving a purposeful, systematic, less time-consuming, and knowledge-based method. Furthermore, this QbD approach is applied as a theoretical prediction to determine the appropriate variables that affect the manufacturing process of the quantum dots simultaneously from the least number of trials[33].

The primary objective of the current research is to detect Hg2+ through its ability to decrease the fluorescence intensity of LUQDs' “turn off”. Then, the fluorescence can be easily recovered upon the addition of sulfide so that the “turn-off” state is switched back to the “turn-on” state. Thesynthesized dotshave been successfully applied to estimate amounts of Hg2+ and S2- in the environment and food samples. The designed approach illustrates no need for derivatization, extraction, or heating. Hence, the developed study indicated extreme simplicity of workup. Compared with the previously reported methods, the established analytical approach provides good sensitivity with unmatched simplicity.

Red lentils were obtained from local commercial stores. Acetic acid (96 %), L-tryptophan, phosphoric acid, boric acid, sodium hydroxide, zinc powder, and metal salts, including mercuric chloride, sodium sulfide, sodium chloride, potassium chloride, lead acetate, magnesium chloride, nickel chloride, cobalt (II) chloride, cadmium sulfate, and copper sulfate were obtained from Sigma – Aldrich (Steinheim- Germany). Water samples, including Nile River waters, tap waters, and sewage waters, were collected locally from the Nile River, laboratory tap, and water assessment unit in Mansoura, Egypt, respectively. Canned Tuna fish samples were purchased from the local market. For all experiments, all solutions were prepared from ultra-pure water, and all chemicals were of Analytical Grade, purchased from commercial suppliers, and no further purification was required for their use.

The fluorescence intensity was analyzed using a spectrofluorometer (Cary Eclipse) at a voltage of 800 V. FTIR Spectrophotometer (IS10 Nicolet, USA) was employed to perform Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) experiments in the rangeof 400 to 4000 cm−1 using KBr pellet method. Shimadzu UV-1601 double beam Spectrophotometer (Japan) was operated forUV spectraprocessing. A centrifuge (model 2-16P, SIGMA, Germany) was used. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX, Oxford-Xmax 20, England) and scanning electron microscope (SEM, Jeol JSM, 6510LV, Japan) were used to examine the composite state of the sample. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) data was collected using an X-raydiffractometer(GNR-APD-2000-PRO, Italy).A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) was used to characterize the morphological propertiesof LUQDs (EOL, JSM −2100-Tokyo, Japan) at 200 kV acceleration voltage. A Smart R-750MR(K) Microwave (2450 MHz, 900 W, Egypt) was used for the fabrication of LUQDs. The factorial design was conducted adopting Minitab® Statistical Software (Release 16, State College, Pennsylvania, USA).

A Microwave (R-750MR(K), 2450 MHz) was used to obtain LUQDs from Red Lentils and urea. Red lentils were ground using a home miller, and 1 g of the powder was mixed with 1 g of urea and dissolved in 5 mL of ultra-pure water in a beaker. The beaker was exposed to microwave irradiation for 10 min at 900 W. The collected brown residue was sonicated with 20 mL of water for 15 min and then filtered using Whatman Filter paper. The filtrate was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min. The centrifuged solution was filtered through a 220 nm syringe filter. The filtrate was kept in an amber-colored bottle at 4 °C for further characterization and future use. Scheme 1 demonstrates the fabrication of LUQDs from red lentils and urea and sensing applications.

Britton Robinson buffers (pH 2.1–12.0) were used during the study. Briefly, it was prepared using acids, including boric, acetic, and phosphoric acids (0.04 M each). The pH of this combination was adjusted to the desired value using 0.2 M NaOH. A final stock solution of each of HgCL2 and Na2S (1000.0 µM) was obtained using ultra-pure water, while a stock solution of LUQDs was prepared by transferring 10 mL of the faint brown dots into a 100 mL volumetric flask, then completing to the mark using ultra-pure water. Both working solutions were further stored in the refrigerator; thus, they remained stable for one month.

The determination of the quantum yield (QY) of the as-prepared dots performed via standard method using Tryptophan (TR) in the pure water of pH 7 with reported QY: 0.12 at 278 nm excitation wavelength [34,35] as reference standard according to Equation (1):
(1)
ϕ
L UQDs
=
ϕ
TR
×
F
L UQDs
F
TR
×
A
TR
A
L UQDs
×
η
L UQDs
η TR
2

Where,
ϕ
L UQDs
and
ϕ
TR
represent the quantum yield of LUQDs and standard reference, respectively. F indicates the emission peak integration area. A stands for the absorbance value, while η denotes the refractive index of the solvents.

To avoid self re-absorption of the fluorescence of LUQD and tryptophan during analysis, they were prepared in concentrations with absorbance less than 0.1. The measurements of the dots and the reference were accomplished under the same parameters [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence measurement of Hg2+ and S2-
ce:section.ce:para: Aliquots of Hg2 + solution with different concentrations (0.5–70.0 μM) were transferred into a series of 10-mL measuring flasks containing 0.5 mL of Britton Robinson buffer (BRB of pH 7) and 70 µL of LUQDs, then the volume was completed to the mark with water. After mixing, the fluorescence intensity was measured at 360 nm after excitation at 300 nm. A calibration curve was plotted between the quenched FL intensity and Hg2+ concentration (µm) to obtain a regression equation.

For S2-, several concentrations starting from 10.0 to 150.0 µm were added to 15.0 µm Hg2+, 1 mL of BRB of pH 6, and 70 µL of LUQDs, completing volume to 10.0 mL using ultra-pure water. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at 360 nm (λex = 300 nm). The relative fluorescence intensity (RFI) was plotted vs. S2- concentration (µm) to construct the calibration curve and perform the regression analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Hg2+ and S2- in water Samples
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, river water (collected from Nile River banks, Mansoura, Egypt), sewage water, and tap water were tested for sensing of both Hg2+ and S2-. Initially, the water samples were exposed to centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 20 min and then filtered. Different concentrations of Hg2+(20.0, 30.0, and 50.0 µM) and S2- (50.0, 70.0, and 90.0 µM) were added to the water samples. The same experimental settings under section “2.6” were followed. The found content was calculated through the corresponding regression equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Hg2+ in canned Tuna Fish
ce:section.ce:para: The oil content was completely drained out from the can, and the remaining meat was blended carefully in a domestic food processor. A homogenized spiked sample (2 g) was transferred into a 100 mL Erlenmeyer flask. Subsequently, 1, 10, and 5 mL of concentrated HCl, HNO3, and H2SO4, respectively, were slowly added to the sample. For complete dissolution, the flask was heated using a steam bath. The solution was cooled and filtered. The resulting solution was further transferred to a volumetricflask (25 mL) and completed to volume with distilled water [37]. Finally, Hg2+ was detected in the blank and spiked Tuna samples with various final concentrations (0, 10.0, 30.0, and 50.0 μM) using the LUQDssystem by following the procedure defined in section “2.6” and monitoring the intensity of the fluorescence.The nominal content was calculated using the corresponding regression equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental design in the fabrication of LUQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, we focus on obtaining higher quantum yield (QY) sulfur and nitrogen-doped carbon dots from red lentils, so we studied the dots prepared from red lentils, urea, and (red lentils + urea) together in a ratio of (1:1). According to QY values, it was found that the highest QY was obtained only by using a combination of red lentils and urea (Fig. S1A). Next, it was necessary to study (red lentils: urea) at different ratios 1:1, 1:2, 2:1, 1:3, and 3:1. From Fig. S1B, an elevated QY response was obtained at ratio of 1:1 and 1:2. The selected ratio during our study was (1:1).

To obtain the best sensing performance of LUQDs, the factors affecting the microwave-assisted method were first studied. These variables include microwave power (540, 700, and 900 W), irradiation time (2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 min), and water volume (3, 5, and 7 mL). The highest QY of the dots was obtained at microwave power (700 and 900 W), irradiation time (8 and 10 min), and (5 and 7 mL) of water. Furthermore, the application of the Quality-by-Design concept is useful for helping to find a suitable strategy during the fabrication of dots with a shorter study time. The factorial design can provide the main effect, interaction, and optimization plots assuring the optimum operating conditions required to attain a high throughput of sensing dots, as depicted in Fig. 1 (A-C) and Supplementary Table S1. The Pareto Chart is also a beneficial tool for determining the parameter settings that result in the best possible QY, Fig. 1D. In summary, the optimum operating conditions applied during the fabrication of LUQDs are mixing red lentils and urea at a ratio of 1:1 using 5 mL of pure water for 10 min at 900 W.

The as-prepared dots exhibit mono-dispersity with uniform size distribution over the range of 3 to 8 nm through the application of high resolution-transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM), as indicated in Fig. 2A. To further analyze the surface structure of LUQDs, Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer was used [38]. Fig. 2B shows the FTIR spectrum of the functional groups of LUQDs; there is a characteristic broad peak at 3437 cm-1corresponding to N–H and O–H stretching vibrations, while a sharp peak at 1641 cm−1 represents the C = C bond. The peaks observed at 529 and 2932 cm−1 are attributed to C–H, whereas the bands at 1479 and 2097 cm−1 are related to C = O and C–N, respectively. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was performed to identify the types and contents of elements of the fabricated dots. Fig. 2C shows EDX analysis of LUQDs, confirming that the C and O content are 48.1 % and 25.9 %, which illustrates that LUQDs are mainly based on carbon. The N and S contents were 17.0 % and 9.0 %, respectively, which further verifies that LUQDs are heavily doped with S and N. As can be seen in Fig. 2D, a scanning electron microscope (SEM) displays the elemental components of the dots. While the proportion of S was relatively not high (9 %), its distribution was higher than that of N, reflecting that the main functional group on the surface was sulfur, demonstrating achieving our goal of synthesizing S-rich CDs capable of reacting selectively with Hg2+, as indicated in Fig. 2 D. Also, X-ray diffraction (XRD)provides essential and basic information about the material, including d-spacing, crystal size, crystal structure, and composition. As can be seen, Fig. 2E displays the XRD pattern of the fabricated LUQDswith a sharp diffraction peak at 2θ value of 28.19° which is close to the (002) lattice spacing of graphite-type materials, further confirming the crystalline structure of the carbogenic core [39].

To explore the optical properties of the prepared dots, a cuvette containing the faint brown solution of LUQDs was exposed to UV light of 365 nm indicating blue fluorescence emitting property of the dots (Fig. 2F). The fluorescence spectrum of LUQDs exhibits the redshift in the range of 280–380 nm, and there is no regular pattern with increasing the excitation wavelength, confirming excitation-dependent emission behavior of the dots (Fig. 2G). The high emission intensity of LUQDs was obtained at 360 nm upon excitation at 300 nm (Fig. 2F). The quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized dots was investigated and found to be 14.7 %, which is superior to most reports for synthesizing CDs from natural sources. Previously published CDs were fabricated from red lentils by hydrothermal method at 200 °C for a time period of 5 h with QY of 13.2 % [40]. This in fact reflect the advantages of the proposed method in easily and economically manufacturing CDs at lower time and temperature with a higher QY.

In order to use the as-prepared LUQDs for fluorescent detection of Hg2+or other possible metals, solutions of Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Na+, Pb2+, K+, Cd2+, and Ba2+at 50 μM concentrations are added to LUQDs at different pH values (3,5,7,8) and their fluorescence intensities are recorded. According to Fig. 3 A, the results exhibit the unique ability of Hg2+ at pH 7 to cause an extreme and obvious decrease in the emission intensity of the dots over other metals at different pH levels. These results point out the specific interactions and selectivity between Hg2+ and LUQDs, mainly at pH 7. From experimental data, LUQDs have the potential to detect Hg2+ without any interference in real samples. Under optimized experimental conditions, the fluorescence intensity of LUQDs is gradually reduced after adding increasing concentrations of Hg2+ achieving linearity over the range of 0.50–70.0 μM, as shown in Fig. 3B.The linear correlation (r = 0.9997) between the quenched FL intensity and Hg2+ concentration was demonstrated in Fig. 3C. The limit of quantitation (LOQ) and limit of detection (LOD) were estimated to be 0.19, and 0.06 μM, respectively, as indicated in Supplementary Table S2. The accuracy of the method was very good as depicted in Supplementary Table S3.

Table 1 illustrates a comparison between the prepared sensor behavior and previously reported CDs using numerous doping atoms (P, N, B, and S) for Hg2+ fluorescence sensing. Regarding sensitivity, the proposed method showed better sensitivity than many methods [41–43] and comparable sensitivity to other methods [44–48]. Despite the fact that some methods[49–51] showed better sensitivity, these methods suffer from long reaction times of up to 24 h [51], complexity, sophisticated and expensive instruments, high temperature, are time-consuming, and need further modification after synthesis. Additionally, Table 1 proves that as-prepared LUQDs exceed the previously reported sensing probesin terms of easy preparations, less time and temperature consumption, availability of precursors, and reliability in environmental applications for the estimation of Hg2+.

Initially, it is essential to investigate the optimal concentration of Hg2+ required for the best sensitive assay of S2-. If the concentrations of Hg2+ are high, S2- will bind to the free Hg2+ in the solution instead of Hg2+ on the surface of LUQDs, leading to an obvious decrease in the sensitivity of S2-. Different concentrations of Hg2+ (5.0, 10.0, 15.0, 20.0, 25.0, 30.0, and 40.0 μM) were included during the study. As shown in Fig. 4 A, 15.0 μM of Hg2+ was selected as the working concentration owing to the highest FL intensity recovery rate upon the addition of S2-.

Numerous experimental factors were investigated, including buffer pH, volume of buffer, and incubation time. During the study, BRB was utilized at different pH values (5, 6, 7, and 8). It was found that pH 7 (Fig. 3A) and 6 (Fig. S2A) are the most suitable ones for the subsequent steps of estimation of Hg2+ and S2-, respectively. The volume added of buffers was also examined from 0.5 mL up to 2.5 mL. Aliquots of 0.5 ml of pH 7 and 1 mL of pH 6 were chosen because they show high fluorescence signals, as indicated in Fig. S2B. Moreover, the incubation time was optimized at different time points from 1 min to 25 min. It was deduced that the interaction between LUQDs and Hg2+ and between S2- and Hg2+-LUQDs was achieved instantly and remained stable for at least 25 min (Fig. S2C). According to the results obtained after 25 min of incubation, increasing the incubation time did not affect the interactions betweenLUQDs and Hg2+ and between S2- and Hg2+-LUQDs. Therefore, 1 min was selected as the optimal incubation time.

Under optimal conditions, the gradual addition of Hg2+ leads to a subsequent decrease in the FL intensity of LUQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 3B. In order to determine the sensing mechanism responsible for quenching, the absorbance band of Hg2+ and the excitation and emission spectra of LUQDs were recorded and assembled in one graph (Fig. S3A) to examine their degree of overlap. From Fig. S3A, it was found that there is no spectral overlap, and hence, the inner filter effect (IFE) was excluded. Subsequently, the Stern-Volmer Equation (Eq.2) was applied to elucidate the possible mechanism at three different temperatures (301, 313, and 323oK)[59].
(2)
F
o
F
= 1 +
K
sv
Q
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
Q
F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher (Hg2+), respectively.
Kq and Ksv refer to the quenching rate constant and the Stern Volmer quenching constant, respectively.

[Q] refers to the quencher concentration.
τ 0
is the average lifetime (10−8 s)[60].

It was found that the values of Ksv decrease gradually with increasing temperature, and thus, it was concluded that the main mechanism of quenching is the static quenching effect (Fig. S3B)[61]. Furthermore, the absorbance spectra of LUQDs in the presence and absence of Hg2+ were recorded. Fig. S3C shows that the absorption maxima were changed with the addition of Hg2+ in LUQDs solution, indicating the formation of a ground state complex with the fluorophore. This observation further substantiates the quenching mechanism to be static in nature [62]. The mechanism of sensing of S2- is through its reaction with Hg2+ in the Hg-LUQDs complex, resulting in freeing LUQDs and turning its fluorescence on, as indicated in Fig. 5 .

Under the optimal detection conditions, three samples of Hg2+ (20.0, 30.0, and 50.0 µM) and S2- (50.0, 70.0, and 90.0 µM) were measured three times on the same day and on three consecutive days as per ICH guidelines [63]. As indicated in Supplementary Table S4, the values of RSD % are less than 2 %, demonstrating the high precision of the designed study.

Additionally, the robustness of our proposed method was confirmed by performing minor variations in the studied experimental parameters, including the volume of LUQDs (70 µL ± 10 µL) and pH of the used BRB buffer. As expected, the results in Supplementary Table S5 illustrate that no significant changes were detected in the analytical response during the quantification of both Hg2+ and S2- ions.

The designed system was applied for the determination of Hg2+ without interference from common ions (Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Na+, Pb2+, K+, Cd2+, and Ba2+), as indicated in Fig. 3A. The selectivity was attributed to the high content of S in CDs due to the use of red lentils which have a rich content of sulfur amino acids. Hence, the sulfur atoms reacted specifically with Hg2+ rather than other metals. It was also performed for estimation of S2- ion without interference from any possible ions (Cu2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Na+, Pb2+, K+, Cd2+, CH3COO–, SO4 2-and Cl-), as illustrated in Fig. 4B, demonstrating the selectivity of suggested methodology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Hg2+ and S2- in water samples
Analysis of Hg2+ in canned Tuna Fish
ce:section.ce:para: Mercury levels in many foods are very low compared to the levels found in Tuna fish. EPA has established a threshold value for mercury with a maximum contaminant level of 0.3 mg/kg(0.3 ppm) in fish[11]. Different concentrations of Hg(II) in canned tuna samples were analyzed to evaluate the applicability of the proposed sensor to real samples. An initial step is based on spiking the samples with diverse volumes of Hg(II), followed by sample digestion, then applying the previously mentioned procedure for fluorescence analysis of the Hg(II). According to the results in Table 3, relative recoveriesranging from 98.70 to 100.90 % were obtained, indicating the applicability of the designed method.

Greenness assessment is a developmental tool for indicating environmental responsibility and resulting energy-saving profiles. In this study, there are numerous ways to measure the environmental friendliness of the proposed method. Initially, the complex green analytical procedure index (Complex-GAPI) is a newly developed software that covers every step of an analytical process, starting from sample preparation to sample assembly, transportation, storage, and ultimate sample analysis. The GAPI-designed schematic diagram is enlarged by the Complex-GAPI scale by involving an additional hexagonal field at the bottom. The hexagonal part represents the pre-analysis processes [66,67]. The value of the E-factor is obtained from the number of reagents, solvents, and waste products generated; a lower value indicates less waste production and environmental friendliness, as displayed in Fig. S4A.

Moreover, another developed tool, AGREEprep, was applied to evaluate the greenness of sample preparation.It is a round pictogram with a score value over the range of 0–1 in the center. The method displays environmental friendliness performance as the value is near to or equal to 1 with a central green color. It is composed of 10 areas; each area reflects greenness principles of sample preparation with different lengths, where the length represents the weight assigned [68,69]. The results of AGREEprep evaluations indicate that the greenness of application of the designed system in water samples is higher than that of canned tuna due to the use of concentrated acids during the preparation of canned tuna, as presented in Fig. S4B and S4C, respectively. The AGREEprep value is 0.9 in water samples and 0.74 in canned Tuna, but the value is still acceptable, which indicates the greenness of the designed system.

Finally, the AGREE approach was performed to assay the greenness of the developed method. It is characterized by a clock-shaped graph with 12 segments. Each segment reflects 12 principles of greenness evaluation. The color of the pictograph varies from green, yellow, and red, corresponding to high, medium, and low greenness values [70]. The plot of the AGREE approach shows a value of 0.83 and a green color reflecting the greenness of our study, as in Fig. S4D. All discussed assessment tools confirm a higher degree of greenness of our developed sensing method.

A simple, sensitive, and green Microwave-assisted technique was established to fabricate sulfur-rich LUQDs from red lentils as C and S-source and urea as N-source. The QbD strategy was implemented to determine the optimum conditions needed to obtain CDs with the highest QY. A faint brown dot with 14.7 % QY with maximum emission at 360 nm was successfully synthesized and fully characterized. LUQDs display higher stability, blue photoluminescence, easy preparation from available sources, high water solubility, and excitation-dependent emission. Novel LUQDs were applied as an effective and selective “Turn off-on” fluorescent probe for quickly tracking traces of Hg+2 and S2- with LOD values of 0.06 and 1.35 µM, respectively. Firstly, Hg+2 ion quenches the emission intensity of the fabricated dots “turn off” based on static effect. Then, the quenched fluorescence can be quantitatively restored by S2- owing to its stronger binding ability with Hg+2. LUQDs show higher sensitivity and selectivity for estimation of both Hg+2 and S2- in environmental water samples and Hg+2 only in canned Tuna fish. Compared with other sensors, the designed method offers several advantages of speed, simplicity, sensitivity, and low cost.Moreover, method greenness was confirmed by implementing several metric tools.
This work is funded by Mansoura University (project code number: Mu-phar-23–32).

Shymaa M. Abd Elhaleem: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Fathalla Belal: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yasser El-Shabrawy: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Mahmoud El-Maghrabey: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110658.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
